title: From the American Revolution to Universal Suffrage
gist: Danielle Allen argues that Britain's failed attempt to crush the American Revolution exposed a constitutional crisis within the British empire itself, which led one English statesman, Charles Lennox, Third Duke of Richmond, to conclude that only broader suffrage could check royal power and keep imperial rule compatible with liberty.

insights.claim: Britain's attempt to crush the American Revolution exposed a constitutional crisis at the heart of its empire, leading one English statesman to conclude that only broader suffrage could check royal power, tame corruption, and keep imperial rule compatible with liberty.
insights.speaker: Danielle Allen
insights.so_what: Frames the American Revolution as a catalyst for domestic British democratic reform, not just a colonial loss — useful historical framing for pieces on how external shocks force internal institutional reform.
insights.quote: One English statesman saw in it proof that only broader suffrage could check royal power, tame corruption, and ensure that imperial rule remained compatible with liberty

insights.claim: Charles Lennox, the Third Duke of Richmond, was serving as Britain's ambassador to the Court of Versailles by October 1765 at age 30, already five years into a bitter feud with King George III, in a post beneath his abilities and ambitions.
insights.speaker: Danielle Allen
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific historical figure (Richmond) whose suffrage argument the piece builds on, providing the biographical anchor for the thesis.
insights.quote: Largely unknown to contemporary scholars, Richmond was already five years into a bitter feud with King George III
